flashcards.front: Define the CIA triad in the context of mobile device security and give one example of compromising each element.
flashcards.back: Confidentiality ensures data is accessible only to authorized users (e.g., encrypting local storage with AES-256). Integrity guarantees data remains unaltered (e.g., using code signing to prevent app tampering). Availability ensures authorized access when needed (e.g., implementing remote wipe to protect data if device is lost).

flashcards.front: Explain the three main mobile device deployment models: COPE, CYOD, and BYOD. Which offers the highest security and why?
flashcards.back: COPE (Corporate-Owned, Personally-Enabled) gives the company full control; CYOD (Choose Your Own Device) lets employees choose from approved devices; BYOD (Bring Your Own Device) uses personal devices with limited management. COPE offers highest security as IT departments maintain full control over hardware, OS updates, and can enforce containerization.

flashcards.front: What is SEAndroid (SELinux) and how does it strengthen mobile security architecture?
flashcards.back: SEAndroid (Security-Enhanced Android) implements mandatory access control (MAC) via SELinux, restricting apps and processes to least privilege access. It uses security contexts (labels) and policies to prevent privilege escalation attacks, limiting damage even if an attacker compromises an application.

flashcards.front: Explain the OWASP Mobile Top 10 (2024) categories M1 and M2. Provide one real-world attack example for each.
flashcards.back: M1 (Improper Credential Usage) involves hardcoded API keys or improper session management (e.g., token leakage via app logs). M2 (Inadequate Supply Chain Security) involves malicious libraries or tampered SDKs (e.g., pre-installed malware in third-party app stores). Both can lead to data breaches and unauthorized backend access.

flashcards.front: What is certificate pinning and how does it protect against man-in-the-middle (MITM) attacks in mobile apps?
flashcards.back: Certificate pinning embeds the server's expected SSL/TLS certificate or public key directly in the app, rejecting connections if the certificate changes. This prevents attackers with malicious CA certificates from intercepting encrypted traffic, even if the device is compromised.

flashcards.front: Compare containerization vs. MDM-only approaches for enterprise mobile security. Which is preferred for high-security environments?
flashcards.back: MDM-only manages the entire device (wipe, lock, policy enforcement), while containerization creates isolated workspaces (Samsung Knox, Microsoft Intune) separating corporate from personal data. Containerization is preferred for high-security environments as it protects corporate data even if the personal portion of the device is compromised.

flashcards.front: Under GDPR and HIPAA, what are the mandatory mobile device security requirements for handling personal/health data?
flashcards.back: GDPR requires encryption (Article 32), breach notification within 72 hours, and data portability/deletion rights. HIPAA mandates encryption for PHI at rest and in transit (Safe Harbor rule), access controls, and audit trails. Both require risk assessments and incident response plans covering mobile device loss/theft scenarios.

flashcards.front: A financial services company implements biometric authentication (fingerprint) for mobile banking. What are three security weaknesses and how should they be mitigated?
flashcards.back: Weaknesses: (1) Biometric data stored in insecure locations—use TEE/SE for storage; (2) Presentation attacks (fake fingerprints)—implement liveness detection; (3) No fallback mechanism—require biometric + PIN for high-value transactions (multi-factor). Additionally, implement certificate pinning, root/jailbreak detection, and session timeouts to defend against device theft and malware.
